levels.title: Everyday Basics
levels.description: Foundational words used in everyday Italian.

words.definition: A large body of water, often referring to the open sea or the coastal water near the land.
Something recently created, discovered, or acquired, or something unused.
A building for human habitation, especially one that is lived in by a family or small group of people.
Word referring to an unspecified object, idea, action, or matter, often used in questions.
The end part of the human arm beyond the wrist, including the palm, fingers, and thumb.
The period of twenty-four hours or the time of light between sunrise and sunset.
Word used to describe that something is done in a good, correct, or satisfactory way.
The supreme being, creator, and ruler of the universe, worshipped in monotheistic religions.
The pronoun used by a speaker to refer to himself or herself.
Small amount, quantity, or degree, often suggesting that something is not enough.
Pronoun referring to a male person previously mentioned or easily identified.
Pronoun referring to a female person previously mentioned or easily identified.
words.info: "Lei" also functions as the polite, formal "you" for strangers or respected individuals, distinct from the informal "tu." It can be used to address both men and women.

levels.title: Numbers
levels.description: Italian numbers and some examples of their usage.

words.definition: The cardinal number representing the quantity of two units.
The cardinal number representing the quantity of three units.
The cardinal number representing the quantity of four units.
The cardinal number representing the quantity of five units.
"Circa" (about, approximately) | "sei" (six) | "mesi" (months). Describes an approximate duration of six months.
The cardinal number representing the quantity of seven units.
"Dodici" (twelve, the cardinal number 12) | "Mesi" (plural of mese, month, each division of the year). The complete expression refers to the full cycle of one year.
"Quattordici" (fourteen, cardinal number 14) | "Mesi" (plural of mese, month). The complete expression refers to a duration of fourteen months.
The cardinal number representing the quantity of fifteen units.
"Circa" (about, approximately) | "due" (two) | "anni" (years). Describes an approximate duration of two years.
The cardinal number 122, composed of "cento" (hundred) + "venti" (twenty) + "due" (two).
The cardinal number 123, composed of "cento" (hundred) + "venti" (twenty) + "tré" (three).

levels.title: People And Family I
levels.description: Common Italian terms for people and family members.

words.definition: A male child, typically from birth to adolescence. Also used generically to refer to a child. Examples: "Aspettiamo un bambino tra tre mesi" (They're expecting a child in three months) | "Il bambino stava imparando ad abbattere gli alberi" (The boy was learning to cut down the trees).
A female child, typically from birth to adolescence. Example: "La bambina ha ricevuto un nuovo vestito per il suo compleanno" (The girl got a new dress on her birthday).
words.info: Unlike "bambino" (boy/child) which can be used generically to refer to a child, the word "bambina" can only be used to refer to a female child.

words.definition: Female sovereign ruler of an independent state or wife of a king.
A noble title for a man who owns land and holds power in the feudal hierarchy, often used loosely for a very wealthy or influential person.
A female parent, especially a woman who has given birth to a child, but the can also refer to a woman who raises and cares for a child.
A female domestic worker responsible for cleaning, cooking, and other household chores, often in service to a family or noble household.
A married woman in relation to her spouse.
A married man in relation to his spouse.
A male sibling sharing at least one parent with another person.
Plural feminine form of "sorella" (sister). Female siblings sharing at least one parent.
The mother of one’s parent, used as a term of address for an older woman.
"Nonna" (grandmother, mother of one's parent) | "Materna" (pertaining to the mother). Refers to one's mother's mother.

levels.title: People And Family II
levels.description: Common Italian terms for people and family members.

words.definition: Belonging to a niece (daughter of one's brother or sister) or to a granddaughter (daughter of one's child).
words.info: Italian word "nipote" can mean niece, nephew, or grandchild. The articulated preposition "della", a fusion of "di" (of/from) and "la" (singular feminine "the"), clearly indicates that a subject here is a female, so it refers to either the speaker's niece or granddaughter.

words.definition: "Giongo" (nephew) | "Mio" (my). Expression used to address or talk about one's nephew in close family settings.
words.info: The word for "Nephew" used outside of the close family settings is "nipote"

words.definition: Son of one's aunt or uncle.
words.info: The daughter of one's aunt or uncle is "cugina".

words.definition: Young male between childhood and adolescence.
words.info: The word for young girl is "giovinetta".

words.definition: Belonging to an uncle, brother of one's parent.
Exiting from the location of one's aunt.
Plural form of "donna" (woman). Multiple adult human females.
Polite address for girl or unmarried woman.
Person of legal adult age.
Professional medical practitioner or healer.

levels.title: Time And Recurrence
levels.description: Essential terms to talk about time, days, and regular events.

words.definition: One of twelve parts into which the year is divided, approximately 30 days.
Plural of "mese" (month). Used when talking about two or more months.
Period of seven consecutive days, typically from Sunday to Saturday.
"Ogni" (each, every) | "Giorno" (day, 24-hour period). Daily recurrence.
"Ogni" (each, every) | "Quindici" (fifteen) | "Giorni" (days). Biweekly recurrence.
Something that happens exactly one time every week.
"Due" (two) | "Anni" (plural of "anno", year). Period of two full years.
Something that has happened three months before the referenced time.
Something that has happened on three separate occasions.
Passage of existence but can also mean atmospheric conditions.
The early part of the day, from dawn until noon.
Time of day from around noon to early evening (approximately 12 PM to 6 PM)

levels.title: Nature And Elements
levels.description: Words describing natural elements, weather, and the world.

words.definition: The name of the planet we live on, but can also mean dry land or upper layer of earth's surface where plants grow
A perennial plant with an elongated stem or trunk supporting branches and leaves.
Large area covered with trees and undergrowth.
Reproductive structure of a plant, often colorful.
A large natural stream of water that flows across the land into a sea, lake, or another river.
A violent disturbance in the atmosphere, often involving strong wind with heavy rain, snow, thunder, lightning, or rough sea.
An intense weather event dominated by very strong winds, often together with heavy snow, rain, or hail.
Plant with three leaflets.
words.info: Despite its name, it can sometimes have 4 leaflets, but that's very rare.

words.definition: Grey powder that is left behind after something like wood or a fire burns.
Illumination enabling vision.
Thin stream of light.
"Bosco" (wooded area, smaller grove) | "Sacro" (sacred, holy). A consecrated or mystical wooded area.

levels.title: Emotional States
levels.description: Italian words for emotions and the observable states of those experiencing them.

words.definition: An intense, unpleasant emotion triggered by the perception of danger, threat, or harm—whether real or imagined.
Strong wish or craving for something to happen or be obtained.
Gentle, reflective form of sorrow or pensive sadness.
Eager interest in learning or discovering something new.
State of enjoyment, delight, or satisfaction.
Intense fury or displeasure from frustration or injustice.
Description of a subject experiencing joy: bright eyes and smiling face.
Description of a subject experiencing sorrow: downcast eyes and slumped posture.
Description of a subject experiencing timidity: averted gaze and hesitant speech.
Description of a subject experiencing fear: wide eyes and trembling hands.
Inner emotional stirring, often positive or profound.
words.info: Don't confuse it with the English word "commotion," which means physical or social uproar like a riot or crowd noise.

words.definition: State of comfort or emotional relief, especially after distress.

levels.title: Familiar Things I
levels.description: Basic objects from everyday life.

words.definition: Wax stick that burns for light.
Collection of printed pages.
Type of clothing worn on the upper body, usually over a shirt or dress, to keep warm or look neat.
Rigid container, usually made of cardboard or wood, used to store or carry small objects.
Furniture with multiple drawers.
Furniture for writing work.
Enclosed space in a house where people sleep or keep their personal things.
Portable hand-held device used to create airflow for cooling.
Structure for indoor fire.
Plural form of "pantofola" (slipper). Comfortable house shoes.
Reflective glass surface.
A small glass or plastic container for liquids like perfume or medicine.

levels.title: Familiar Things II
levels.description: Basic objects from everyday life.

words.definition: Dark colored formal garment worn for special occasions or mourning.
Loose garment worn for sleeping.
System for organizing days, weeks, months, and years.
Hand coverings for warmth or protection.
Small container holding drinking water.
Small table placed beside a bed.
Flat surface forming the bottom of a room.
Group of things tied or held together.
Foot covering for protection and walking.
words.info: The plural form of that word is "scarpe"

words.definition: Images and stories seen in the mind during sleep.
Open agricultural or natural fields. In urban context, public squares or open spaces that were originally used for farming.
"Vetri" (plural of "vetro" - glass) | "Rotti" (masculine plural form of "rotto" - broken). Shards or fragments of broken glass, typically from shattered windows or bottles.

levels.title: Familiar Things III
levels.description: Basic objects from everyday life.

words.definition: Plural form of "busta" (envelope). Paper containers for letters or documents.
Animal flesh used as food.
Products or items available for sale or transport.
words.info: The plural form of that word is "merci"

words.definition: Plural form of "foglia" (leaf). Fflattened, typically green outgrowth from a plant stem.
Facial expression showing pleasure or amusement.
Community event usually organized to celebrate or commemorate something.
Derived from "tramontare" (to go beyond the mountains). The time in the evening when the sun disappears below the horizon. Can also symbolize decline or the end of something.
An opening in a wall with glass that lets in light and allows seeing outside. Example: "Dalla finestra minuscola, Alice vide il giardino magico" (From the tiny window, Alice saw the magical garden).
Wall-mounted fixture that supports a candle, bulb, or other light source, typically featuring a reflective backplate to direct and amplify the illumination.
A fast and bright discharge of electricity in the sky during a storm. Figuratively, it can also mean something or someone that moves very quickly.

levels.title: Conversation Connectors
levels.description: Connective expressions that create seamless movement between sentences or conversational turns.

words.definition: Interrogative adverb used to request reasons or explanations, and as a conjunction to introduce a cause.
Conjunction introducing a conclusion, result, or logical consequence based on previous statements.
Interjection used to begin, continue, or prompt a conversation, often expressing readiness or challenge.
Adverb indicating a minimum amount, concession, or partial satisfaction in contrast to what is desired.
Adverb expressing recognition of an unwelcome fact or outcome, often implying personal regret or disappointment.
Fixed phrase used to introduce an illustration, case, or example that clarifies a statement.
Adverbial expression meaning that something occurs abruptly or without prior indication.
Phrase meaning 'where are you?', used to inquire about the physical location of a person or object.
Adverb used to confirm truth, express emphasis, or indicate genuine surprise or belief.
Adverb expressing possibility, uncertainty, or doubt about the truth or occurrence of something.
Singular interrogative pronoun or adjective used to identify, specify, or select one option among several. Example: 'Quale fiore scegli?' (Which flower do you choose?)
Plural interrogative pronoun or adjective used to identify, specify, or select multiple options among several. Example: 'Quali fiori scegli?' (Which flowers do you choose?)

levels.title: The Wonderland Vibe
levels.description: Advanced adjectives and adverbs capturing the tense, chaotic atmosphere throughout Alice in Wonderland and beyond.

words.definition: Past completed form of "decapitare" (to decapitate). Describes having the head cut off by execution.

Past completed form of "decollare" (to take off). Primarily describes an aircraft or rocket that has taken off from the ground, or figuratively, a project, career, or initiative that has suddenly launched or gained rapid momentum/success.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), this verb implies one's head being taken off by execution.

words.definition: Present form of "tremare" (to tremble). Describes someone or something shaking involuntarily, typically from fear, cold, excitement, or weakness.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), characters tremble before the Queen's wrath.

words.definition: Present form of "sibilare" (to hiss). Describes a sharp, hissing or whispering sound. Example: "Vento sibilante" (hissing wind).
In a hurried or rushed manner. Example: "Uscì frettolosamente." (He left hastily).
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), the White Rabbit was running hastily past Alice upon their first encounter

words.definition: Adverb derived from "rabbioso" (rabid or furious) + "-mente" (the Italian suffix used to form adverbs from adjectives, equivalent to "-ly" in English). Describes an action performed with intense anger or fury.
Feminine form of "spaventoso" (frightening). Causing intense fear or horror.
Describes extreme hopelessness, anguish, or frantic action.
"Quasi" (almost, nearly) + "impazzito" (compelted form of "impazzire", to go mad). Describes someone on the verge of madness or extreme frenzy.
Describes a profound sense of horror, dread, or shuddering revulsion, often provoked by something shocking or gruesome.

"Calpestìo" (heavy, thudding footsteps or trampling sound) | "frettoloso" (hurried, frantic) | "per la scala" (along the stairs—up, down, or chaotic motion). Evokes the Rabbit's panicked, echoing rush in Alice's dreamscape.
words.alice_context: Captures the White Rabbit's obsessive 'I'm late!' sprint, filling the air with desperate clamor.

words.definition: "Uno" (indefinite article used for masculine nouns, equivalent to a/an in English) | "Smarrimento" (profound mental wandering, as if lost in fog) | "Angoscioso" (viscerally distressing, soul-clenching). Conveys raw, existential vertigo amid the absurd.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), this phrase emphasizes the profound disorientation triggered by Wonderland's absurdity, chaos, and madness, causing Alice's sense of reality to unravel.

levels.title: Colors & Qualities
levels.description: Italian words for colors and qualities; some double as names.

words.definition: Achromatic color corresponding to the perception of maximum lightness with no hue, typically produced by light that contains, reflects, or scatters nearly all visible wavelengths.
Achromatic color corresponding to the perception of maximum darkness with no hue, typically produced by light that contains, absorbs, or scatters nearly no visible wavelengths.
words.info: Singular feminine: "nera"; plural masculine: "neri"; plural feminine: "nere"

words.definition: Bright primary color in the visible spectrum, positioned between green and orange, typically perceived when light has a dominant wavelength of about 575–585 nanometers.
words.info: The masculine form of that word is "giallo"

words.definition: Italian female given name symbolizing purity and illumination.
words.info: Historically, the 'bianchi' (Whites) were one of two political factions in late 13th-century Florence, opposed to the 'neri' (Blacks). The split originated in Pistoia’s Cancellieri family, where descendants of Bianca Cancellieri sparked the Guelph divide via a children's feud.

words.definition: Plural form of "colore" (color), referring to visual perceptions of light differentiated by wavelength and hue, typically encompassing all tones visible to the human eye in the spectral range between approximately 380 and 750 nanometers.

Delicate chromatic tone corresponding to a light blue hue, positioned between blue and cyan in the visible spectrum, typically perceived when light has a dominant wavelength of about 490–500 nanometers. Also an Italian female given name.
words.info: The spelling of this word is the same for masculine and feminine versions.

words.definition: Deep blue-green hue corresponding to a color range positioned between blue and green in the visible spectrum, typically perceived when light has a dominant wavelength of about 480–490 nanometers.
words.info: Diminutive of "turchese" (turquoise), often evokes the tone of deep seawater.

words.definition: Triumph or success, historically symbolizing radiant achievement and dominance, metaphorically linked with illumination and brilliance of spirit. Also an Italian female given name.
Expression denoting exalted honor or radiant renown achieved through exceptional actions, often metaphorically associated with brightness and majesty. Also an Italian female given name.
Singular feminine form of "luminoso" (luminous). Emitting or reflecting significant visible light, perceived as radiant or glowing in appearance.
Color or surface exhibiting low saturation and brightness, often resulting from a reduction in chromatic intensity or diffuse reflection of light.
words.info: Often applied to skin tone, light shades of pigments, or faintly illuminated objects.

words.definition: "Chiari" (plural masculine form of "chiaro" - light) | "Bruciati" (plural masculine past participle of "bruciare" - burnt). Phrase used to describe pale, sun-scorched or aged desaturated colors.
words.info: Singular masculine: "Chiaro bruciato"; describes sun-damaged hair, fabrics, or surfaces.

levels.title: Commands and Requests
levels.description: Sophisticated expressions of orders, pleas, directives, suggestions, and requests.

words.definition: "Conducete" (voi imperative of "condurre" - to lead) | "la" (direct object "her") | "quì" (here). A formal command, typically from a position of authority directing a group to escort a feminine subject to the present location.

"Porta" (tu imperative of "portare" - to carry) | "-mi" (me) | "subito" (immediately) | "i" (plural masculine definite "the") | "guanti" (gloves). An informal imperative demanding immediate delivery of gloves, usable as urgent order or casual request among friends.
"Levate" (voi imperative of "levarsi" - to remove oneself) | "-vi" (plural reflexive). A formal command, typically from authority ordering a group to clear passage immediately.
"Tu" (singular informal "you" for emphasis) | "mi" (me) | "segua" (Lei formal imperative of "seguire" - to follow). A formal command for polite insistence from authority directing you to follow.

"Per" (for, preposition) | "carità" (charity, mercy, compassion). Emotional interjection humbly begging for compassion/mercy, typically shouted in panic, fear, or desperate supplication when facing harm or injustice.

translations: For pity's sake let me go
words.definition: "Per carità" (for mercy's sake) | "lascia" (tu imperative "lasciare" - to let/allow, io lascio, loro lasciano) | "-mi" (direct object me) | "partire" (infinitive - to leave/depart). Desperate informal plea combining emotional mercy cry + permissive imperative construction urgently begging for release from confinement or restraint.
"Scusate" (voi imperative of "scusare" - to excuse) | "tanto" (so much). A polite formal request seeking forgiveness from a group.
"Credete" (voi imperative of "credere" - to believe) | "lo" (direct object it). A formal command, typically from authority ordering a group to accept a statement as truth.
"Corri" (tu imperative of "correre" - to run) | "a" (to) | "casa" (home, house). An informal ordering or urging someone to run home immediately.
"Fai" (tu imperative of "fare" - to do/make) | "lo" (it). Informal command ordering performance of specific action previously mentioned.
Informal imperative command urging a single individual to turn their attention toward something that speaker refers to.
words.note: "Guarda" is the singular informal (tu) form of guardare. Singular formal (Lei): "Guardi"; plural informal (voi): "Guardate"; plural formal (Loro): "Guardino"; speaker's (io): "Guardo".

words.definition: Informal imperative command urging a single individual to start talking or give verbal response.
words.note: "Parla" is the singular informal (tu) form of parlare. Singular formal (Lei): "Parli"; plural informal (voi): "Parlate"; plural formal (Loro): "Parlino"; speaker's (io): "Parlo".

levels.title: Occurences and Turns
levels.description: Words describing curved shapes, bends, and turning moments.

words.definition: Primarily denotes "time" or "turn" in Italian, referring to an instance, occasion, or moment. Can also be used to denote a curved architectural structure such as a vault or arch. Example: "Ogni volta che guardo quel dipinto, inizio a sognare a occhi aperti Firenze" (Every time I look at that painting, I start daydreaming of Florence).
Describes something with a rounded, arched, or bent shape. Example: "Un ramo curvo" (A bent branch).
words.info: "Curvo" is singular masculine form. Singular feminine: "Curva"; plural masculine: "Curvi"; plural feminine: "Curve".

words.definition: "Prima" (first) | "volta" (time, occasion). Refers to an initial occurrence or experience of an event, activity, or situation. It captures the novelty and often accompanying emotions like excitement, nervousness, or discovery. Example: "È difficile dimenticare la tua prima volta nelle Alpi Svizzere" (It's hard to forget your first time in the Swiss Alps).
"Contro" (against, preposition) | "la" (singular feminine "the") | "volta" (vaulted ceiling, arched roof). Denotes forceful contact or opposition against an arched ceiling or overhead curve.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), Alice's head struck against the roof of the hall after she ate the cake, which caused her to grow enormously tall.

words.definition: Adverb derived from the verb "penzolare" (to dangle or swing). Describes objects hanging loosely downward in curved, swinging motion.
"Chinare" (incline) | "la" (singular feminine "the") | "testa" (head). Describes bending or turning the head downward.
"Rivoltò" (past completed singular masculine form of "rivolgere" - to turn) | "cautamente" (carefully). Describes a deliberate, gentle rotation or flipping motion.
Plural masculine form of "rotondo" (round, circular). Something fully rounded or circular in shape.
words.info: "Rotondi" is plural masculine form. Singular masculine: "Rotondo"; singular feminine: "Ronda"; plural feminine: "Ronde".

words.definition: Describes turning back or returning to previous state, place, or moment.
"Ritornare" (return) | "a" (to) | "lei" (to her). Describes going back to a woman or feminine noun after being away.
To move or pass from one side to the other of a space, area, or obstacle.
To begin something once more after a pause or interruption.

levels.title: Body Parts and Sizes
levels.description: Terms for body parts, size-related words, and physical movement.

words.definition: Uppermost part of the body containing the brain, eyes, etc.
Top part of body or leader.
Physical structure of a person or animal.
Front part of the upper body.
Primarily refers to a person's physical height or build, but can also be used figuratively to denote moral, intellectual, or social stature or prominence.
Part of face above eyes.
Adjective for something of considerable size or importance.
Adjective describing extended length.
Joint between upper and lower arm.
A thigh that is uncovered or exposed, without clothing covering it. Example: "L'abito corto le lasciava la coscia scoperta" (The short dress left her thigh exposed).

"Così" (so) | "piccina" (little/tiny). Diminutive feminine form of "piccolo" (small). Describes something extremely small in size, often endearingly. Example: "Alice divenne così piccina da poter camminare attraverso la porticina" (Alice became so tiny that she could now walk through the door).
"Un" (a/an) | "Braccio" (arm). Upper limb from shoulder to hand.
words.info: In natural Italian speech, the 'n' sound in the article 'un' often sounds like 'm' before certain consonants, which may confuse learners into hearing a non-existent article 'um'. This is simply a result of nasal assimilation in pronunciation, not a separate word.

levels.title: Actions and Movement I
levels.description: Italian verbs and movement-related phrases to describe daily activities and story events.

words.definition: Past form of the verb "trovare" (to find). Something that has been discovered or located.
Past form of the verb "ritornare" (to return). Someone who has come back to a previous state or place.
Past ongoing form of the verb "restare" (to remain). Describes staying in a place or state that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
Action of consuming food through chewing and swallowing.
words.info: Mangio implies "I eat" from its -io ending. No explicit "io" (I) word is needed before the verb—Italian drops the subject pronoun when the verb conjugation clearly shows who is acting.

words.definition: Past form of the verb "partire" (to leave). Someone or something that has gone away from the place the verb refers to.
"Avvicinatevi" (come closer) + "Dunque" (then, therefore). Urgent invitation for a group to step nearer.
words.info: "Avvicinatevi" is the formal plural you (voi) imperative form of the verb "avvicinarsi" (to approach/come closer). Singular informal (tu) form is "Avvicinati!"

words.definition: To begin or set in motion an action, process, or event; to take the first step in doing something. A versatile verb used for starting activities, situations, or developments.
Downward motion into a place or space already mentioned. Describes an action of descent or entry from a higher point.

"Cacciare" (to chase, to drive out) | "-lo" (him) | "via" (away). Expel or send someone off forcefully. Describes deliberate dismissal or removal of a person, often expressing irritation, authority, or defensive reaction. Example: "Volevano cacciarlo via subito" (They wanted to chase him away immediately).

Past completed form of "cacciare" (to chase) with "via" (away). Describes someone or something already expelled or removed from a place entirely. It emphasizes the outcome—the separation being complete. Example: "Fu cacciato via dal gruppo" (He was chased away from the group).
Action of fleeing quickly to avoid capture or danger.
words.info: The -i ending clearly marks it as singular informal you (tu form) of the verb "scappare" (to escape, to run away). The singular formal (lei) form of that verb would be: "scappa." The plural formal (Loro) form would be: "scappano." The plural informal (voi) form is: "scappate."

words.definition: "Saltando" (jumping) | "come" (like) | "matti" (mad people, crazy). Present ongoing form of "saltare" (to jump). Describes rapid, repeated, and energetic movements resembling wild or uncontrolled excitement. Example: "I bambini correvano e ridevano saltando come matti" (The children ran and laughed, jumping like mad).

levels.title: Actions and Movement II
levels.description: Italian verbs and movement-related phrases to describe daily activities and story events.

words.definition: Past completed singular formal (lei) form of "fuggire" (to flee). Marks the moment someone made a swift getaway, often in haste or under pressure.
words.info: "Fuggì" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "Fuggisti". Plural informal (voi) form is "Fuggiste". Speaker's (io) form is "Fuggii".

words.definition: "Partire" (to leave) | "per forza" (by force). Denotes a departure that's involuntary, driven by force, necessity, or outside compulsion—like being made to leave due to eviction, duty, or crisis—rather than a free choice.

"Cercò" (past completed singular formal (lei) form of "cercare" - to try) | "di" (to) | "aprire" (to open) | "-lo" (enclitic pronoun "it"). Refers to attempting to open something up, typically with deliberate effort amid potential difficulty or resistance.
words.info: "Cercò" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "Cercasti". Plural informal (voi) form is "Cercaste". Speaker's (io) form is "Cerciai".

words.definition: "Doveva" (past obligation singular formal (lei) form of "dovere" - must) | "partire" (to leave). Expresses a past obligation or necessity to depart, often implying a scheduled or unavoidable trip ahead.
words.info: "Doveva" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "Dovevi". Plural informal (voi) form is "Dovevate". Speaker's (io) form is "Dovevo".

words.definition: "Voleva" (past ongoing singular formal (lei) form of "volere" - to want) | "ritornarci" (return there) | "ci" (there/to it). Expresses a strong past desire or wish to return to that specific place or thing again.
words.info: "Voleva" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "Volevi". Plural informal (voi) form is "Volevate". Speaker's (io) form is "Volevo".

words.definition: "Seguivano" (past ongoing plural informal (voi) form of "seguire" - to follow) | "dieci cortigiani" (ten courtiers). Ten courtiers trailing behind the subject, often in a formal procession or entourage
words.info: "Seguivano" is the plural informal (voi) form. Singular formal (lei) form is "Seguiva". Singular informal (tu) form is "Seguivi". Speaker's (io) form is "Seguivo".

translations: Was descending slowly down the chimney
words.definition: "Scendeva" (past ongoing singular formal (lei) form of "scendere" - to descend) | "adagino" (gently, slowly) | "lunghesso il camino" (along the chimney). Depicts a gentle, gradual descent alongside or down the chimney, evoking a slow, careful slide.
words.info: "Scendeva" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "Scendevi". Plural informal (voi) form is "Scendevate". Speaker's (io) form is "Scendevo".

words.definition: "Le" (to her, indirect object) | "si" (reflexive pronoun) | "avventarono" (past completed plural form of "avventarsi" - to rush at) | "addosso" (upon). Describes a group suddenly hurling themselves violently toward a female subject with aggressive intent. Example: "I nemici le si avventarono addosso" (The enemies rushed at her).
"Stringendo" (gerund/ongoing form of "stringere" - to tighten/grip/squeeze) | "Sempre" (always/continuously) | "Più" (more) | "Forte" (strong/hard). Gripping with ever-increasing force.
Past ongoing singular feminine form of "progredire" (to progress). Describes steady forward movement or development over time.
Movement toward the speaker or a specific place. Used for both physical arrival and approaching in time or situation.
Plura form of "capitombolo" (tumble). multiple headlong tumbles or somersaults, often implying chaotic, rolling falls with the head leading the way.

levels.title: Everyday Expressions I
levels.description: Common Italian phrases and words used in daily conversation and storytelling.

words.definition: Marks the immediate present moment, signaling something happening right this instant or about to start immediately.
Sharp cutoff, expressing that a situation, action, or excess has reached its limit and must end right now.
Past completed plural informal (voi) form of "capire" (to understand). Signals full comprehension or acknowledgment of what's been said.
"Che" (what) | "significa" (present singular formal (lei) form of "significare" - to mean). Straightforward request for clarification or explanation.
"Che" (what) | "fa" (present singular form of "fare" - to do/make). Casual, shorthand way of asking about someone's current activity or state.
"Che" (what) | "dice" (present third person singular of "dire" - to say). Casual, neutral conversation starter inviting someone to speak.

"Che" (what) | "cosa" (thing) | "stai" (present singular informal (tu) form of "stare" - are) | "facendo" (present ongoing form of "fare" - doing) | "qui" (here). Direct question about someone's current action at this specific location.
"Certo" (surely/certainly) | "ritornerò" (future singular from of "ritornare" - to return). A confident assurance of returning.
"Come" (how) | "sono" (present singular from of "essere" - to be) | "felice" (happy). Expresses profound joy or satisfaction.
Describes physical strength, intense volume, or forceful action depending on context.
Comparative form of "buono" (good). Indicates superior quality, condition, or preference over something else.
Absolute absence of anything; complete lack or zero quantity of substance or matter.

levels.title: Everyday Expressions II
levels.description: Common Italian phrases and words used in daily conversation and storytelling.

words.definition: "Ecco" (here/behold). Points out or presents someone or something that has just arrived or become visible.

Past completed form of "fare" (to do/make). As an adjective, it describes something constructed, completed, or suited to purpose, like a task or person marked by readiness and maturity in form and function. As a noun, it refers to an occurrence, affair, or tangible truth, like a historical event, often carrying legal weight as a verifiable reality shaping outcomes and contrasting empty words with solid actions.
words.info: Can also mean "indeed" as an adverb.

words.definition: "Capito" (past completed form of "capire" - understand) | "questo" (this). Crisp acknowledgment of grasping a key idea, argument, or instruction, signaling full comprehension in the moment.
words.info: From Alice context: acknowledging an explanation.

words.definition: Present singular formal (lei) form of "dire" (to say). Straightforward verb pinning down spoken words or claims, often with a casual edge of reporting what someone utters.
words.info: "Dice" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu): "dici"; plural informal (voi): "diciate"; speaker (io): "dico"; plural (noi): "diciamo"; plural informal (voi present): "dite"; plural (loro): "dicono"; speaker's (io): "dico".

words.definition: "Niente" (nothing) | "di male" (wrong/bad). Reassures that an action or situation causes no harm or offense.

"Meglio" (better) | "di" (of, than, from, about, by, with, in, to, for, per; here 'than' in comparison) | "così" (thus, so, like this, in this way, as such). Pithy expression of peak satisfaction or superiority, implying perfection with no room for improvement.
"Non" (not) | "lo" (it) | "sai" (present singular informal (tu) form of "sapere" - know). States or questions lack of knowledge about something specific.
Indicates the current location or immediate proximity.

translations: that's how it came to be
words.definition: "Così" (thus, so, in this way) | "accadde" (past completed form of "accadere" - to occur, befall). Conveys a narrative unfolding where events fell into place just as described, marking inevitable or fated outcomes with storytelling finality.
"Forse" (perhaps, maybe, possibly) | "ritornerò" (future form of "ritornare" - to return, come back). Expresses tentative hope or uncertainty about one's future reappearance.
"Mi" (to me) | "disse" (past form of "dire" - to say). Recounts a direct, personal communication witht the speaker from the listener's point.
"Non" (not) | "lo" (it) | "dice" (present singular formal (lei) form of "dire" - to say). Indicates that someone refrains from verbalizing something.

levels.title: Everyday Expressions III
levels.description: Common Italian phrases and words used in daily conversation and storytelling.

words.definition: Describes doing something in a light, joyful mood, with visible ease or playful energy, as when someone chats or works with a smile.

Present singular form of "aspettare" (to wait), or a noun meaning outward look or particular side of something. It either expresses actively waiting for someone or highlights one quality of a person, object, or situation.
Indicates something surrounding a point, person, or object on every side, whether in physical space or in a more general sense like reactions around an event.
Plural noun referring to exciting, unusual, or risky experiences that stand out from daily routine, often remembered as stories or turning points.
Base form of a verb meaning to give someone what they hope for or need so they feel at ease, respected, or happy with the outcome.
Describes a person, movement, or situation marked by sharp, jerky, or restless energy, as if shaken by strong emotion or strain.
Refers to a sense of basic respectability and acceptable behavior, matching what people consider clean, modest, or appropriate in a given setting.
Study of fundamental questions about existence, knowledge, and values.
Names something that gives deep, pleasant enjoyment, such as a favorite food, view, or moment that feels especially rewarding.
Refers to a steady belief that someone or something will act reliably or favorably, allowing a person to rely on them without constant worry.
"Poco" (little) | "dopo" (after). Indicates a short time interval following an event.
Entirety or total completeness of something.
words.info: "Tutto" is the singular masculine form. Feminine singular: "tutta"; masculine plural: "tutti"; feminine plural: "tutte".

levels.title: Everyday Expressions IV
levels.description: Common Italian phrases and words used in daily conversation and storytelling.

words.definition: To accept something as true or real without necessarily having concrete or credible evidence for it. Example: "Credo che l'ananas rovini la pizza" (I believe pineapple ruins pizza).
Describes something wicked, harmful, or of poor quality.
words.info: "Cattivo" is singular masculine form. Singular feminine: "Cattiva"; plural masculine: "Cattivi"; plural feminine: "Cattive".

words.definition: Act of kindness or generosity toward those in need, often with compassion or plea for understanding.
State of questioning or lack of conviction about something's truth.
Describes someone mentally unstable or foolishly irrational.
words.info: "Pazzo" is singular masculine form. Singular feminine: "Pazza"; plural masculine: "Pazzi"; plural feminine: "Pazze".

words.definition: Present singular (io) form of "impazzire" (to go mad). Describes the ongoing feeling of losing mental control, extreme frustration, or overwhelming emotion happening right now.
words.info: "Impazzisco" is the speaker's (io) form. Singular informal (tu) form: "impazzisci"; singular formal (lei) form: "impazzisce"; plural (noi): "impazziamo"; plural informal (voi): "impazzite"; plural formal (loro): "impazziscono".

words.definition: Newly received or communicated information about events.
Quality of being in accordance with fact or reality.
To make someone stop talking, often abruptly or forcefully.
Plural noun for silly, pointless, or untrue statements and ideas.

levels.title: Everyday Expressions V
levels.description: Common Italian phrases and words used in daily conversation and storytelling.

words.definition: To bring something to completion or reach the end of an activity, process, or period. Example: "Devo finire questo libro stasera" (I have to finish this book tonight).
Describes intense, savage aggression or cruelty, often in animals or people acting with wild anger.
words.info: "Feroce" is the singular masculine form. Singular feminine: "Feroce"; plural masculine: "Feroci"; plural feminine: "Feroci".

words.definition: The permanent end of all life functions in a person, animal, or plant.
Refers to being by oneself without others, or exclusively one thing without more.
words.info: "Solo" is singular masculine. Singular feminine: "Sola"; plural masculine: "Soli"; plural feminine: "Sole".

words.definition: "Primo" (first) | "momento" (moment). Describes the initial instant when something begins or is first noticed.
Past completed form of "dormire" (to sleep). Describes having rested through sleep that is now finished.
words.info: Singular masculine form shown. Feminine: "Dormita"; plural masculine: "Dormiti"; plural feminine: "Dormite".

words.definition: Past completed form of "pagare" (to pay). Describes money exchanged for goods, services, or debts that is now settled.
words.info: Singular masculine form shown. Feminine: "Pagata"; plural masculine: "Pagati"; plural feminine: "Pagate".

words.definition: To refuse to admit truth or reject a request. Example: "Non posso negare l'evidenza" (I can't deny the evidence).
Official or legal permission to do something, or freedom from normal rules.
A unit of speech or written language, or one's assurance to do something. Example: "Dammi la tua parola" (Give me your word).

levels.title: Narration and Descriptions I
levels.description: Words for storytelling, locations, actions, and descriptive details.

words.definition: Singular formal (lei) past ongoing form of "dire" (to say/tell). Describes telling or saying something that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.

Plural formal (loro) past ongoing form of "dire" (to say/tell). Describes telling or saying something that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
Past completed form of "dire" (to say/tell). Describes a statement or saying that happened once and was completed in the past.
Present ongoing form of "dire" (to say/tell). Describes continuous or ongoing speech or expression.
Obvious, easy-to-understand action. Example: "Ha spiegato chiaramente come preparare la pasta" (She explained clearly how to make pasta).
Within something or indoors. Example: "Entrò dentro per prendere la cassetta degli attrezzi" (He went inside to get the toolbox).
Beyond a particular space or boundary. Example: "Sta piovendo fuori" (It’s raining outside).
At the back of something. Example: "C’è un piccolo giardino dietro quel caffè" (There’s a small garden behind that café).
Higher than or on top of something. Example: "Il libro è sopra il tavolo" (The book is on the table)
Adjective describing great distance. Example: "Il deserto è lontano da qui" (The desert is far from here).
Feminine form of "buono" (good). Describing something pleasant.
Adverb describing poor performance.

levels.title: Narration and Descriptions II
levels.description: Words for storytelling, locations, actions, and descriptive details.

words.definition: Past ongoing form of "piacere" (to be pleasing). Describes something that was liked continuously or habitually in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
words.info: Example: "Mi piaceva il gelato" (I used to like ice cream [but now I don't]). Pay attention to the phrase structure: it's "to me it pleased" instead of "I liked".

words.definition: "Condurre" (to lead/take) in past hypothetical form. Example: "Se i giardinieri conducessero Alice..." (If the gardeners were leading Alice...).
words.info: "Conducessero" is the formal plural (loro) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "conducessi". Singular formal (lei) form is "conducesse". Plural informal (voi) form is "conducessimo". Speaker's (io) form is "conducessi".

words.definition: Past ongoing form of "circondare" (to surround). Describes surrounding that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now. Example: "I fiori circondavano Alice" (The flowers were surrounding Alice).
words.info: "Circondavano" is the formal plural (loro) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "circondavi". Singular formal (lei) form is "circondava". Plural informal (voi) form is "circondavate". Speaker's (io) form is "circondavo".

words.definition: Past ongoing form of "rispondere" (to reply). Describes responding that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now. Example: "Rispondevo alle domande" = "I was answering the questions".
words.info: "Rispondevo" is the speaker's (io) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "rispondevi". Singular formal (Lei) form is "rispondeva". Plural informal (voi) form is "rispondevate". Plural formal (loro) form is "rispondevano".

words.definition: Verb that comes from "parlare" (to speak). Describes speaking happening right now or at the same time as another action.
Adverb referring to the next day. Example: "Ci vediamo domani." (See you tomorrow).
Describes something connected to divinity, reverence, or solemn respect. Used for rituals, persons, or principles held in great honor.
"Aspettò" (he/she/it waited) | "Un poco" (a little). Describes a completed brief waiting action that happened once in the past.
words.info: In natural Italian speech, this phrase quite often sounds like "aspetto un po’"

words.definition: Past ongoing form of "fare" (to do/make). Describes actions that were continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and aren't happening right now.
"Vicino" (near, close by) | "a" (to) | "lei" (her). Indicates literal or figurative proximity to a woman or feminine subject.
Past ongoing singular form of "meritare" (deserve). Signifies that something or someone was deserving of an outcome, reward, or treatment, based on their worth or actions.
Refers to a large or wild animal, often fierce or uncontrollable. Used figuratively for brutal or savage people.

levels.title: Narration and Descriptions III
levels.description: Words for storytelling, locations, actions, and descriptive details.

words.definition: Deliberate act of exacting retribution through payback or justice.
Physical power, courage, or compelling influence over something.
Rapidly ascending projectile driven by explosive propulsion that leaves a luminous trail
"Più" (more) | "forte" (strong). Indicating greater degree in strength, intensity, volume, or force.
Competitive encounter or contest between opposing sides, typically in sports or games, structured as a single event with defined rules and outcomes.
Portion, section, or component derived from dividing a whole, whether material or conceptual.
Plural of "parte" (part). Portions, sections, or components derived from dividing a whole, whether material or conceptual.
Describes something that goes against or faces away from another thing, such as an opposing idea, direction, or result.

Institution or official body with the authority to adjudicate legal disputes. Can also refer to a monarch's or sovereign's residence, or to an open space under the sky that is enclosed on all sides by buildings or walls.

Past completed masculine plural form of "macellare" (to slaughter). Refers to animals that have been butchered or slaughtered, typically for meat. Can also be used in relation to people or animals brutally killed or destroyed.
words.info: "Macellati" is a plural masculine form. Singular masculine: "macellato"; singular feminine: "macellata"; plural feminine: "macellate".

levels.title: Past Reflections
levels.description: Phrases capturing past events, states, and memories.

words.definition: Past ongoing plural form of "godere" (to enjoy). Describes shared pleasure that continued over time in the past, but stopped at some point or its current status is unknown. Example: "Godevamo ogni momento" (We enjoyed every moment).

"Non" (not) | "afferrò" (past completed form of "afferrare" - to grasp) | "nulla" (nothing). Describes a single finished failure to understand or grab something that happened once in the past. Example: "Non afferrò nulla della spiegazione" (He didn't grasp anything of the explanation).

"Era" (past ongoing form of "essere" - to be) | "innamorato" (in love). Past ongoing masculine form describing a romantic affection that continued over time in the past, but it's unknown whether that state is still happening now. Example: "Era innamorato di lei per anni" (He was in love with her for years).

"Era" (past ongoing form of "essere" - to be) | "innamorata" (in love - feminine) | "davvero" (truly). Past ongoing feminine form describing authentic romantic love that continued over time in the past, but it's unknown whether that state is still happening now. Example: "Era innamorata davvero di lui" (She was truly in love with him).

"Indovinò" (past completed form of "indovinare" - to guess) | "subito" (immediately). Describes a single, finished act of quick correct guessing that happened once in the past. Example: "Indovinò subito la risposta" (He guessed the answer right away).

Past ongoing form of "mancare" (to lack). Describes something absent or needed that continued over time in the past, but stopped at some point or its current status is unknown. Example: "Mancava solo una cosa" (Only one thing was missing).

"Non" (not) | "tornò" (past completed form of "tornare" - to return) | "più" (anymore). Single finished event of permanent absence that happened once in the past. Example: "Non tornò più a casa" (He never returned home).
Past completed plural form of "rispondere" (to reply). Describes a group giving their answer as a single finished action in the past. Example: "Risposero all'unisono" (They replied in unison).

Past ongoing form of "scherzare" (to joke). Describes joking behavior that continued over time in the past, but stopped at some point or its current status is unknown. Example: "Scherzava sempre con tutti" (He was always joking with everyone).

"Veramente" (truly) | "era" (past ongoing form of "essere" - to be) | "così" (like that). Confirms a past situation that continued over time exactly as described. Example: "Veramente era così semplice" (It was really that simple).

"Parlava" (past ongoing form of "parlare" - to speak) | "anche" (also). Describes someone speaking in addition to others that continued over time in the past, but stopped or its current status is unknown. Example: "Parlava anche lei" (She was also speaking).

"Cose belle" (beautiful things) | "noi godevamo" (past ongoing plural form of "godere" - we were enjoying). Describes habitual shared enjoyment of lovely experiences that continued over time in the past, but stopped or its current status is unknown. Example: "Cose belle noi godevamo insieme" (Beautiful things we enjoyed together).

levels.title: Future and Conditionals
levels.description: Verbs expressing future actions, hypotheticals, possibilities, and polite wishes.

words.definition: "Mi" (me) | "direste" (you would tell). Conditional form used politely when asking a question or request. Example: "Mi direste dove andare?" (Would you tell me where to go?).

"Fossero" (past hypothetical form of "essere" - to be) | "decapitati" (decapitated). Past hypothetical plural masculine form describing heads cut off that would have happened in a past conditional scenario, but actually did not occur. Example: "Se i giardinieri non dipingessero le rose, fossero decapitati" (If the gardeners didn't paint the roses, they would have been decapitated).

Hypothetical present form of "dire" (to say) used when asking what a group of people would say in a hypothetical situation. Example: "Cosa direste se non funzionasse?" (What would you say if it wouldn't work?).
words.info: "Direste" is the plural informal (voi) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "diresti". Plural informal (voi) form is "direste". Singular formal (lei) form is "direbbe". Plural formal (loro) form is "direbbero". Speaker's (io) form is "direi".

words.definition: Primarily used to express obligation, necessity, or duty to perform an action or behave in a certain way. It may, in some contexts, also serve to convey probability or expectation. Examples: "Devo restituire il prestito." (I have to repay the loan. [obligation]) | "Devi mettere il cappotto in inverno." (You must wear a coat in winter. [necessity]) | "Dovrebbe piovere nel pomeriggio." (It should rain this afternoon. [probability]).
Expresses physical or mental ability to perform an action, permission to do something, or the possibility that something can occur.

Hypothetical form of "svanire" (to vanish). Used to describe something that could disappear, weaken, or lose presence under certain conditions. Example: "Il ricordo svanirebbe col tempo" (The memory would fade with time).

Future form of "zappare" (to dig or to cultivate) for second person singular. Describes an action that will occur or depends on a future condition. Example: "Se lavorerai duro, zapperai la terra più fertile" (If you work hard, you will cultivate the richest soil).
Hypothetical past form of "partecipare" (to participate/take part). Used to express an imagined or counterfactual situation in which someone might have participated but actually did not.
words.info: "Partecipasse" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "partecipassi". Plural informal (voi) form is "partecipaste". Plural formal (loro) form is "partecipassero". Speaker's (io) form is "partecipassi".

words.definition: "Fosse" (were) | "affamato" (hungry). Past or imagined form used to talk about an unreal situation. Example: "Fosse affamato, cucinerebbe qualcosa da solo" (If he were hungry, he would cook something himself).

"Fosse" (had been or were) | "rimasto" (remained). Past completed form referring to something that could have happened but didn’t. Example: "Fosse rimasto ad Anchorage quest'inverno, avrebbe visto l’aurora boreale" (Had he stayed in Anchorage this winter, he would have seen the northern lights).

"Volessero" (they might want) | "dire" (to say). Hypothetical or uncertain form for expressing what someone could mean or be trying to suggest. Example: "Sembrava che volessero dire più di quanto dicessero" (It seemed they wanted to say more than they did).

"Sarebbe" (would have been) | "ritornato" (returned). Past completed form showing a possibility that never took place. Example: "Sarebbe ritornato se il viaggio fosse stato più corto" (He would have returned if the trip had been shorter).
words.info: This phrase appears in some readings of Alice in Wonderland when referring to an unrealized return, but its usage and meaning are broad and apply to any context.

levels.title: Future Threats & Conditions
levels.description: Phrases expressing future possibilities, commands, and dramatic warnings.

words.definition: Future tense third person plural of "decapitare" (to decapitate). Describes heads being cut off as a definite future action. Example: "Decapiteranno tutti i giardinieri" (They will behead all the gardeners).

"Mi" (me) | "farà" (future form of "fare" - to make/cause) | "impiccare" (to hang). Describes someone causing the speaker's execution by hanging as a definite future event. Example: "Mi farà impiccare per questo errore" (He will get me hanged for this mistake).
"Per" (by) | "mio" (my) | "volere" (will/command). Asserts absolute authority over future actions. Example: "Tutto avverrà per mio volere" (Everything will happen by my will).

"Morisse" (past hypothetical form of "morire" - to die) | "di fame" (of hunger). Describes death by starvation as a dramatic past hypothetical warning. Example: "Morisse di fame, non lo aiuterei" (Even if he were to starve, I wouldn't help him).

"Scoperchierebbero" (past hypothetical form of "scoperchiare" - to unroof) | "il tetto" (the roof). Describes violently removing a roof as a past conditional threat that didn't happen. Example: "Scoperchierebbero il tetto se non pagassi" (They would tear off the roof if I didn't pay).
Present hypothetical form of "sapere" (to know). Expresses a nuanced knowledge, usually in uncertain or emotional situations, often infused with a bit of doubt or supposition.
words.info: "Sappia" is the singular formal (lei) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "sappi". Plural informal (voi) form is "sappiate". Plural formal (loro) form is "sappiano". Speaker's (io) form is "sappia".

words.definition: Present informal imperative singular form of "vedere" (to see). Informal command to look at or notice something immediately. Example: "Vedi cosa hai combinato" (See what you've done).

"Proibisce" (present form of "proibire" - to prohibit) | "la macellazione" (slaughtering). States an ongoing rule against killing animals for meat. Example: "La legge proibisce la macellazione in città" (The law prohibits slaughtering in the city).

"Pazzerella" (affectionate feminine "little crazy") | "che" (that) | "sei" (present form of "essere" - to be). Playful scolding acknowledging someone's silly behavior. Example: "Pazzerella che sei, sempre a combinare guai" (Crazy girl that you are, always getting into trouble).

"Ne" (of it/about it) | "scriverò" (future form of "scrivere" - to write) | "uno" (one). Definite future promise to create something in response. Example: "Ne scriverò uno migliore" (I will write a better one).

levels.title: Various Actions
levels.description: Words for describing various kinds of actions.

words.definition: Past ongoing form of "sospirare" (to sigh). Describes sighing that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
Past ongoing form of "gridare" (to shout). Describes shouting or calling out loudly that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
Expresses the act of shedding tears due to pain or emotion. Can occur in any time frame depending on context.
Present ongoing form of "masticare" (to chew). Describes chewing happening right now or at the same time as another action.
Present ongoing form of "crescere" (to grow). Describes growing happening right now or at the same time as another action.
Imagine or daydream. Describes fantasizing that can happen in present, past, or future depending on context.
Present ongoing form of "cangiare" (to change) with object. Describes continuously changing voice tone or pitch happening right now.
"Guardò" (past completed form of "guardare" - to look) + "tutt'intorno" (all around). Describes a completed action of looking in every direction that happened once in the past.
Past completed form of "osservare" (to observe). Describes a completed action of carefully watching the cat that happened once in the past.
Past ongoing form of "domandare" (to ask). Describes asking questions that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point.
Past ongoing form of "singhiozzare" (to sob). Describes sobbing that was continuously happening in the past, but stopped at some point and isn't happening right now.
Past completed form of "entrare" (to enter). Describes a completed action of going into a place that happened once in the past.

levels.title: Creatures and Characters
levels.description: Animals and figures from Alice's fantastical encounters.

words.definition: Domestic animal known for producing an iconic purring sound.
words.info: The feminine form of that word is "gatta"

words.definition: "Gatta" (female cat) | "Inferocita" (enraged). Furious feline.
Strong, often mocking term for someone extremely cowardly.

"Porcellini" (little pigs) | "D'India" (of India). Small rodents native to South America, not India; the name derives from a misnomer—the word "India" might actually be misused to refer to the Guinea coast of West Africa, possibly because the animals were shipped via Guinea or resembled Indian pigs.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), these animals were spectators during the mockery of a trial where the Knave of Hearts (Fante di Cuori) was accused of stealing the Queen's tarts. Both guinea pigs cheered during the Hatter's testimony and were immediately suppressed: officers put each one headfirst into a large canvas bag, tied it up, and sat on the bags.

words.definition: Long-necked wading bird with pink feathers.
"Fenicònteri" (flamingos) | "Viventi" (living). Describes actual, living flamingos, often imagined in playful or exotic settings.
"Fante" (jack, knave) | "Di cuori" (of hearts).
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), the Knave of Hearts (Fante di cuori) was accused of stealing the Queen's tarts.

words.definition: Masculine plural form of "coniglio" (rabbit). Small burrowing mammals with long ears and timid behavior.
"Tre" (three) | "Giardinieri" (gardeners). People who tend gardens.
"I" (masculine plural "the") | "Principini" (little princes) | "Reali" (royal). Describes young royal children of noble birth.
"Dieci" (ten) | "Soldati" (soldiers). Military personnel.
"Armati" (armed) | "Di bastoni" (with sticks, with batons, with clubs). Equipped with heavy sticks used as weapons.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie) the word "bastoni" means the suit of Clubs in Italian playing cards.

levels.title: States and Actions
levels.description: Words describing conditions, movements, abilities, and purposeful activities in daily life.

words.definition: Something stationary, motionless, or steadfast, like an unmoving object or resolute stance.
Describes the state of possessing life, energy, or animation in beings or experiences.
Singular feminine form of "selvaggio." (wild). Refers to something untamed or fiercely unbound by civilization.
words.info: "Selvaggia" is singular feminine form. Masculine singular: "Selvaggio"; masculine plural: "Selvaggi"; feminine plural: "Selvagge".

words.definition: Plural masculine form of "gremito" (crowded). Refers to crowded, packed, or teeming spaces filled to capacity with people or things.
words.info: "Gremiti" is plural masculine form. Singular masculine: "Gremito"; singular feminine: "Gremita"; plural feminine: "Gremite".

words.definition: Having great depth, intensity, or insight, often describing ideas, emotions, or influences that go far beyond the superficial. it can also describe physical features, like the depths of an ocean or abyss.
words.info: "Profondi" is plural masculine form of "profondo." Singular masculine: "Profondo"; feminine singular: "Profonda"; feminine plural: "Profonde".

words.definition: "Guardare" (to look) | "la" (her/it). Refers to the act of directing one's gaze intentionally toward a woman or a feminine object.
words.info: "Guardarla" is the singular form used for both informal (tu) and formal (lei). Plural informal (voi) form is "guardarvi". Plural formal (loro) form is "guardarle".

words.definition: "Rimessi" (past completed plural masculine of "rimettere" - put back) | "in ordine" (in order). Items restored to proper arrangement after chaos.
words.info: "Rimessi" is plural masculine form. Singular masculine: "Rimesso"; singular feminine: "Rimessa"; plural feminine: "Rimesse".

words.definition: "Scompigliava" (past ongoing form of "scompigliare" - to dishevel) | "tutto" (everything). Action of throwing everything into disorder.
Past ongoing form of "circondare" (to surround). Describes continuous enclosing action around an object or person that was happening in the past but stopped.
To perceive with the eyes or to understand something mentally. Can also indicate anticipation of future events.
To make something cooler, renew, or restore freshness.
To compose text using letters or symbols on a surface.

levels.title: States and Qualities
levels.description: Words expressing conditions, moods, and characteristics.

words.definition: Refers to something different or additional compared to what was already mentioned. Example: "Prendo un altro biscotto" (I'll take another cookie).
Describes something turned completely over or reversed. Example: "Il mondo sembrava capovolto" (The world seemed upside down).
Charming or attractive in an endearing or delicate way. Example: "Quel gattino è davvero bellino" (That kitten is really cute).
Diminutive form of "bene" (well). Describes a moderate or acceptable state of health or condition. Example: "Come stai? — Benino, grazie" (How are you? — Fairly well, thank you).
Eager to know or learn something. Example: "È un bambino curioso" (He's a curious child).
Completely without hope; full of despair. Example: "Era in una situazione disperata." (She was in a desperate situation.)
words.info: The masculine form of that word is "disperato"

words.definition: Complete absence of sound.
Feminine plural form of "nuovo"; recently made or discovered.
words.info: Singular masculine: "nuovo"; singular feminine: "nuova"; plural masculine: "nuovi"

words.definition: Feminine form of "crudo"; Most often means raw, unfinished, or unprocessed, especially when used in relation to food like meat, fish, or vegetables that has not been cooked at all, as in "carne cruda" (raw meat). It can also describe something or someone emotionally raw, harsh, or cruel.
words.info: Singular masculine: "crudo"; plural masculine: "crudi"; plural feminine: "crude"

words.definition: Past completed masculine form of "ferire" (to wound). Describes someone who has received a physical or emotional injury. Example: "Il ferito aveva bisogno di cure immediate" (The wounded man needed immediate care).
words.info: Feminine form: "ferita"; plural masculine: "feriti"; plural feminine: "ferite"

words.definition: Describes rude or disrespectful behavior, especially from someone younger. Example: "Quel bambino è troppo impertinente" (That child is too cheeky).
Describes someone or something so changed that it cannot be identified. Example: "Era così stanco da essere irriconoscibile" (He was so tired he was unrecognizable).

levels.title: Changes and Conditions
levels.description: Words for transformations, states, and resulting situations.

words.definition: "La" (the) | "verità" (truth). Quality or state of being in accordance with fact or reality.
A false statement made deliberately to deceive. Example: "Hai detto una bugia" (You told a lie).
The act of falling or sudden decline from a higher position. Example: "Una caduta improvvisa" (A sudden fall).
Past completed masculine form of "cambiare" (to change). Describes something made different from before. Example: "Tutto era cambiato" (Everything had changed).
Past completed feminine form of "ingiallire" (to turn yellow). Describes something that has become yellow through age or change. Example: "La pagina era ingiallita" (The page was yellowed).
words.info: Singular masculine: "ingiallito"; plural masculine: "ingialliti"; plural feminine: "ingiallite"

words.definition: Past completed feminine form of "dissimulare" (to disguise). Describes something intentionally concealed. Example: "La tristezza era dissimulata" (The sadness was disguised).
words.info: Singular masculine: "dissimulato"; plural masculine: "dissimulati"; plural feminine: "dissimulate"

words.definition: "Cosí" (so) | "Ingiallita" (yellowed). Emphasizes extreme yellowing through age or decay. Example: "La foto era cosí ingiallita" (The photo was so yellowed).

To cause something to become smaller in size. Example: "La bottiglia misteriosa impiccolì Alice fino a farla passare sotto la porta" (The mysterious bottle made Alice small enough to pass under the door).
Expresses complete confidence something is true. Example: "Certamente capisco" (I certainly understand).

Past completed feminine form of "rovesciare" (to overturn). Describes something turned over or reversed. Example: "L'obiezione è stata rovesciata dal giudice" (The objection was overruled by the judge).
words.info: Singular masculine: "rovesciato"; plural masculine: "rovesciati"; plural feminine: "rovesciate"

words.definition: Unsteady physical movement or hesitation between choices.
words.info: "Vacillare" is the base form. Present tense: singular formal (lei/loro) form is "vacilla", singular informal (tu) form is "vacilli", speaker's (io) form is "vacillo", plural informal (voi) form is "vacillate".

words.definition: Present form of "vacillare" (to waver or stagger). Describes movement that is unsteady, as if about to fall.
words.info: "Vacilla" is the singular formal (lei/loro) or plural informal (voi) form. Singular informal (tu) form is "vacilli". Speaker's (io) form is "vacillo".

levels.title: Alice's Wonderland Scenes
levels.description: Words from Alice's garden, transformations, and surreal encounters.

words.definition: An enclosed outdoor space filled with plants, flowers, and paths. Example: "Alice corse nel giardino dove crescevano fiori parlanti." (Alice ran into the garden where talking flowers grew.)
"Tanti" (plural masculine of "tanto" meaning "many" or "a lot of" when quantifying countable nouns) | "Fiori" (plural of "fiore" (flower). The complete expression means numerous/many flowers.
A structure with steps or rungs used for climbing up or down. Example: "La scala scendeva senza fine nel buio" (The staircase went endlessly into the dark).
Large fungus growing on ground or tree.
words.info: Central transformation object in Alice's journey.

words.definition: A dense row of bushes forming a boundary or natural wall. Example: "Una siepe dividia i due giardini" (A hedge separated the two gardens).

Diminutive form of "casa" (house). A very small house, cottage, or hut. Example: "La casettina nel giardino aveva finestre a forma di cuore." (The little house in the garden had heart-shaped windows.)
Diminutive form of "pasticcio" (pastry). A small sweet cake or confection. Example: "Sul tavolo c'era un pasticcino che diceva 'Mangiami'". (On the table was a little cake that said 'Eat me').
"Stava" (was standing) | "Vicino" (near) | "All'ingresso del giardino" (at the garden entrance). Describes position close to a garden doorway.
"Paese" (country, land) | "Delle Meraviglie" (of wonders).
Plural of "meraviglia" (wonder). Extraordinary, magical, or astonishing thing.
"Grosso" (large) | "Fungo" (mushroom) | "Vicino a lei" (near her). Describes a big mushroom located close to a female subject.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), this refers to the giant mushroom Alice finds that causes her dramatic size transformations when she eats from its sides.

words.definition: "Colorendo" (coloring, ongoing action) | "Quelle rose" (those roses). The act of painting or dyeing specific roses.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), the scene depicts playing card soldiers frantically painting white roses red to avoid the Queen's wrath.

levels.title: Diminutives and Reflections
levels.description: This level blends the concrete and the contemplative — from diminutives to words that capture various mental processes.

words.definition: Consecrated building for Christian worship, like a cathedral or chapel. Example: "Quella chiesa è bellissima" (That church is beautiful).

Diminutive of 'chiesa'; small village church or chapel, often charming. Example: "La chiesetta di montagna sembrava un gioiello tra le nevi delle Alpi" (The little mountain church looked like a jewel among the Alpine snow).
words.info: Single diminutive form (-etta suffix) common for rural or quaint churches.

words.definition: Primarily refers to a physical dwelling place, but often carries an emotional sense of "home" as a place of belonging. Example: "Torna a casa presto" (Come home early).

"La" (singular feminine "the") | "casettina" (little house) | "del" (of the) | "coniglio" (rabbit). Rabbit's small dwelling or hutch. Example: "Il coniglio entrò nella sua casettina." (The rabbit entered its little house).
words.info: Double diminutive (-ettina): casa → casetta → casettina. Intensifies smallness.

words.definition: Physical organ in the skull that controls bodily functions and automatic processes such as breathing. It processes signals to enable thought and action. Used literally and figuratively to represent intelligence or decision-making. Example: "Usa il cervello prima di agire!" (Use your brain before acting!).

Abstract concept representing brain activity, including consciousness, thoughts, emotions, and perceptions. It enables reasoning, imagination, and self-awareness, often likened to software on the brain's hardware. Unlike the brain, it can't be touched or measured directly. Example: "Aveva la mente piena di idee" (Her mind was full of ideas).
Awareness of one's existence, surroundings, and actions. Also moral understanding. Example: "Perse la coscienza dopo la caduta" (He lost consciousness after the fall).

Plural form of "considerazione" (consideration). Reflections or judgments formed by reasoning. Example: "Fece diverse considerazioni prima di rispondere" (He made several considerations before replying).
A product of mental activity that arises in the mind. Often carries emotional weight, representing both rational thinking and heartfelt contemplation.

Plural masculine form of "malinconico" (melancholic). Deeply emotional and thoughtful mood relating to quiet sadness. Example: "Avevano sguardi malinconici ma sereni" (They had melancholy yet peaceful looks).

"Non" (not) | "sapeva" (knew) | "più" (anymore) | "lei stessa" (herself). Phrase describing confusion or a loss of personal identity, often caused by emotional turmoil or transformation. Example: "Dopo tanti cambiamenti, non sapeva più lei stessa chi fosse." (After so many changes, she no longer knew who she was.)

To identify or realize something or someone as known, familiar, or true based on memory or observation. Example: "Riconobbe subito la voce dell’amica." (She immediately recognized her friend’s voice.)

levels.title: Inflection
levels.description: Words depicting struggle, danger, and tragic fates.

words.definition: Act of bringing back memories or experiences to mind. Example: "Ricordo bene quella giornata." (I clearly remember that day).
"Molta" (feminine singular "much") | "Curiosità" (curiosity). Describes strong interest in learning or discovering something.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), captures Alice's defining trait that drives her to follow the White Rabbit and explore the surreal Wonderland.

words.definition: Clouded mental state following shock, fatigue, or surprise. Example: "Provò un leggero stordimento quando udì la notizia." (He felt a light dizziness when he heard the news).

Indicates opposition, contrast, or physical proximity against something. Examples: "Lancaster combatté contro York nelle Guerre delle Due Rose" (Lancaster fought against York in the Wars of the Roses).
Feminine form of "infermo" (sick). Chronically ill, frail, or infirm—often implying a long-term, debilitating, or permanent weakened state due to illness, age, or disability.
words.info: Singular masculine: "infermo"; masculine plural: "infermi"; feminine plural: "inferme". Less common in everyday speech; used for ongoing conditions.

words.definition: Feminine form of "malato" (ill). Sick or ill in a general sense, often temporary—from minor ailments like a cold to acute but recoverable diseases.
words.info: Singular masculine: "malato"; masculine plural: "malati"; feminine plural: "malate". Common everyday term for any illness.

words.definition: Situation or circumstance likely to cause serious harm or damage.
The quality or state of being hard to do, understand, or overcome.
Building where people are kept as punishment or while awaiting trial.
Plural of "prigioniero" (prisoner). People held captive or imprisoned.
Refers to a deceased person or to a lifeless state caused by injury, violence, accident, or other fatal circumstances.
Loss or impairment to material, immaterial goods, integrity, or function, often caused externally.

levels.title: Descriptive Scenes
levels.description: Italian phrases capturing colors, sensations, spaces, cleanliness, and everyday objects.

words.definition: To tint or stain objects with red color through deliberate application.
words.info: In the context of Alice in Wonderland (Le Avventure d'Alice nel Paese delle Meraviglie), the  playing card soldiers were frantically painting white roses red to avoid the Queen's wrath.

words.definition: "Campane" (plural feminine form of "campana" - bell) | "Sante" (plural feminine form of "santo" - holy). Sacred or consecrated church bells used in religious ceremonies, tolling to mark significant moments. Example: "Le campane sante suonavano solenni all'alba" (The holy bells rang solemnly at dawn).

"Camera" (feminine singular form of "camera" - room) | "In" (in) | "Attesa" (feminine singular form of "attesa" - waiting). Space designated for people awaiting someone or something specific. Example: "Aspetta nella camera in attesa" (Wait in the waiting room).
"Cameretta" (small room) | "assai" (very) | "pulitina" (diminutive feminine of "pulita" - clean). Describes a tidy, cozy, and modestly kept little room.
Feminine singular form of "deserto". Portrays a space that's abandoned or devoid of people and activity.
words.info: "Deserta" is the sngular feminine form. Masculine singular: "deserto"; masculine plural: "deserti"; feminine plural: "deserte".

translations: White round eye of the dial
words.definition: Describes a white circular clock or gauge face with precision. Example: "Bianco occhio tondo del quadrante indicava mezzanotte" (White round eye of the dial showed midnight).
Refers to each individual sensory experience encountered in sequence. Example: "A ogni sensazione rabbrividiva" (Shivering at every sensation).
Plural feminine form of "banana" (banana). Curved tropical fruits that turn yellow when ripe.
To be sufficient in quantity or quality to meet a need or condition. Example: "Mi basta una tazza di tè" (One cup of tea is enough for me).
Plural masculine form of "bastone" (stick). Rods or staffs used for support or striking. Example: "I bastoni erano robusti" (The sticks were sturdy).
To clear land or purify a feminine object through reclamation. Example: "Volevano bonificarla" (They wanted to reclaim it).
Related to fields, pastures, or pastoral country life. Example: "Strada campestre" (Country road).

levels.title: Sounds and Voices
levels.description: Words describing noises, voices, and auditory experiences.

words.definition: "Grossa" (big, loud) | "Risata" (laughter). Describes hearty, booming laughter.
"Risa" (laughters) | "Sgangherate" (hilarious, uncontrolled). Describes wild, unrestrained group laughter.
"Le" (plural feminine "the") | "Grida" (cries). Collective loud calls or screams.
Light, rapid sound of many footsteps or shuffling movement.
Present ongoing form of "ticchettare". Describes steady, repetitive clicking sound.
